macro_segments.title: Introduction to Avodah Zarah: Context, Purpose, and Terminology of the Tractate

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks: Calendar context and structure of Nezikin
Subject matter of Avodah Zarah: Negotiating pagan and non-Jewish world
Terminology: Goyim, Nochri, Ovdei Kochavim, and Akum; History of censorship and self-censorship
Distinction between non-Jews and idolaters; overlap in rabbinic reality
Thematic divisions of the tractate: First two chapters on negotiating gentile world, middle chapter on idols, last two chapters on wine
Historical and halakhic significance of Avodah Zarah; abundance of Rishonim commentaries
Question about Yerushalmi and differences in perception between Israel and Babylon; dina de'malchuta dina

macro_segments.title: Mishnah on Prohibited Business Transactions Before Non-Jewish Holidays: Three Days Before

micro_segments.title: Mishnah text: Lifnei edeihem, three days before, prohibited transactions (selling, lending, borrowing)
Rabbi Yehuda's dispute: Permissibility of collecting debts because it causes anguish
Response to Rabbi Yehuda: Anguish is temporary; joy follows payment
Rashi's explanation: Business causes joy, increasing non-Jew's happiness on their holiday, leading to thanksgiving to idols
Fundamental concern: Aiding non-Jewish idolatry; Noahide prohibition against idolatry
Three interpretive frameworks: Lifnei iver (aiding their sin); participating in idolatrous acts; participating in pagan culture
Distinction between aiding idolatry and connecting to idolatrous culture; examples of Christmas shopping and participation
Importance of recognizing non-Jews who are not idolaters; limits of lifnei iver principle

macro_segments.title: Tosafot on Sunday Business: Historical Reality of Christian Holidays and Permissive Leniencies

micro_segments.title: Tosafot's question: How do we do business on Sunday, which is their holiday, when the mishna forbids it?
Saint days versus actual holidays; most Christian holidays are saint days, not worship
First answer: Permitted mishum eivah, to avoid engendering non-Jewish hatred
Tosafot's objection to eivah: Why would non-Jews know if we don't come to market?
Alternative: Darchei shalom, cultivating positive interactions with non-Jews
Yerushalmi source: Permission for strangers unknown to non-Jew; permission for known non-Jews as flattery (machanif)
Machanif as politeness and neighborliness, not betrayal of values; example of giving candy to trick-or-treaters
Rabbeinu Tam's approach: Problem only when selling objects used directly in worship
Money is fungible; lending money not directly problematic since money supports priests, not worship itself
Caution when non-Jew explicitly requests money for religious purpose (operier/Pope/clergy)
Rabbeinu Chananel: Supporting priests and monks is indirect support, not direct worship
Key Tosafot: Maybe Christians are not truly ovdei avodah zarah; minhag avoteihem bi'yedeihem (following ancestral custom)
Theological distinction: Christianity as religion vs. individual Christians; wedge between religion and practitioners
Rabbeinu Tam on lending without interest versus with interest; interest causes anger, not joy
Collecting debts on their holidays: Permitted when no document (prevents loss) or possibly even with document

macro_segments.title: Gemara on Aleph vs. Ayin: Edeihem (Days of Destruction) vs. Eideihem (Testimony)

micro_segments.title: Rav and Shmuel debate: Both aleph (eideihem/destruction) and ayin (eideihem/testimony) interpretations are correct
Aleph reading: Days of their downfall (from Deuteronomy 32:35); negative terminology as hope for redemption
Ayin reading: Their testimony against themselves (from Isaiah 43:9); idols will testify against idolaters
Rabbi Yehoshua ben Levi: Mitzvot create testimony; Jews' good deeds testify for them in world to come
Rav Huna: Alternative source for edeihem from Isaiah 45:20 (idols as false testimony)

macro_segments.title: Aggadic Section: Future Judgment of Nations Before God; Rome Comes First

micro_segments.title: Setting: In the future, God brings Torah scroll, invites those who engaged with it to claim reward
Non-Jews rush forward to claim reward; God directs each nation to come separately
Gemara question: Things are not confused before God; answer is for nations' clarity, not God's
Rome enters first as most important; basis in Daniel's vision of beasts; Rome's power spread worldwide
Rule: King and nation judgment; king enters first (honor or before God's anger builds)

macro_segments.title: Aggadic Section: Future Judgment of Nations Before God; Rome Comes First (continued)

micro_segments.title: God's question to Rome: What have you accomplished? Rome's boast: Marketplaces, bathhouses, gold and silver, all for Israel's Torah study
God's response: Idiots! You did it all for yourselves. Marketplaces for prostitutes, bathhouses for indulgence; all wealth is mine
Rhetorical question: Who can testify? Only Torah. Nations leave despondent
Interpretation question: Does testimony verse (Isaiah 43:9) refer to non-Jews or Jews? Answer: Both parts—Jews testify for themselves, non-Jews admit truth

macro_segments.title: Aggadic Section Continued: Persia and Other Nations Attempt Justification; Theological Framing

micro_segments.title: Persia enters next; described as bear-like (eaters, flesh, hairy, restless) from Daniel's vision
Persia's claim: Bridges built, cities conquered, wars waged, all for Israel's Torah
God rejects Persia: Bridges for tax collection, cities for forced labor, wars are mine
Persia's advantage over Rome: Rome destroyed Temple; Persia allowed return and rebuilding
Gemara: Other nations thought they might succeed without direct Jewish contact
Explanation: Rome and Persia mentioned because they maintain dominion until Mashiach; obscure nations not significant
Theological conclusion: Reject gratitude to non-Jewish powers; all accomplishments for selfish motives; all wealth belongs to God

topical_tags.term: Lifnei iver (putting stumbling block before blind)
Rabbeinu Tam's position on selling objects
Testimony and mitzvot (Rabbi Yehoshua ben Levi)
Sheva mitzvot bnei Noach (Seven Laws of Noah)
Dina de'malchuta dina (law of kingdom is law)
Gratitude and appreciation of non-Jewish accomplishments
